text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY SURVEILLANCE VISIT
Head and Neck Oncology Program

ATTENDING: [Medical Oncology Attending]
VISIT TYPE: Follow-up / Surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine 12-month post-treatment surveillance visit. HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, right base of tongue, T2N1M0, status post definitive concurrent Platinol-based chemoradiation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Margaret Chen is a 53-year-old Chinese-American female and former never-smoker who presents today for her 12-month post-chemoradiation surveillance visit. She was diagnosed approximately one year ago with Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition) HPV-positive (p16+, HPV-16 ISH positive) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue. She underwent definitive concurrent chemoradiation completing approximately 12 months ago: intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) using a simultaneous integrated boost technique to 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the primary site and involved right level IIA node, with concurrent Platinol 100 mg/m2 IV every 3 weeks for 2 planned cycles, both administered at full dose.

Treatment course was complicated by grade 3 oropharyngeal mucositis beginning around week 5 of radiation, necessitating percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube placement for nutritional support. She also experienced grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the bilateral neck, grade 1-2 xerostomia, and grade 2 fatigue. Radiation was completed on schedule without interruption, and she received both cycles of cis without dose reduction.

At 12-week post-treatment PET/CT, she achieved a complete metabolic response with no FDG-avid residual disease at the primary site or in the cervical lymph node basin. The PEG tube was removed approximately 5 months post-treatment once adequate oral intake was re-established. Her most recent imaging is a CT neck with contrast obtained at the 12-month mark, reviewed today.

Since her last visit at 6 months post-treatment, the patient reports continuing to do very well. She has returned to full-time work as a high school music teacher and has resumed most of her normal activities. She denies any new throat pain, odynophagia, hemoptysis, otalgia, or unexplained weight loss. She reports no new neck masses or lymph node swelling. No trismus. She does note persistent mild xerostomia, which she manages with frequent water sipping, biotene rinses, and over-the-counter saliva substitute spray; she is also taking pilocarpine 5 mg TID, which she feels provides partial benefit. Diet-wise, she is tolerating a largely regular diet with some self-imposed modifications — she avoids very hard, dry, or chewy foods (crackers, tough meats) due to xerostomia and mild effort dysphagia with large boluses but has no frank aspiration symptoms or choking episodes. She is eating three full meals per day and has regained most of her treatment-related weight loss.

She also notes a sensation of mild bilateral tinnitus that has been present since completing Platinol; this was noted on her 6-month audiogram as mild high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (predominantly 4-8 kHz range bilaterally), consistent with cis ototoxicity. She reports this is not functionally limiting at work, though she is aware of it in very quiet environments. No significant dizziness or vertigo.

She was noted at 6 months post-treatment to have a mildly elevated TSH at 5.8 mIU/L, suspected radiation-induced hypothyroidism. Her levothyroxine dose was adjusted at that time. She reports feeling more energetic since the dose adjustment and denies ongoing fatigue.

She continues using fluoride trays nightly as instructed by dental oncology and follows up with her dentist regularly. She denies any jaw pain, dental pain, or symptoms concerning for osteoradionecrosis.

She denies any constitutional symptoms: no fevers, no chills, no night sweats, no significant unintentional weight loss. Appetite is good.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Mild xerostomia (chronic, stable), mild bilateral high-frequency tinnitus (stable, Platinol-related), minor dietary modifications as above.
Negative: No throat pain, no odynophagia, no hemoptysis, no neck swelling or new masses, no otalgia, no nasal congestion, no hoarseness (voice preserved, important given her profession), no trismus, no facial numbness, no headache, no visual changes, no dyspnea, no chest pain, no abdominal pain, no bowel or bladder changes, no neurological symptoms, no bone pain, no significant fatigue.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing, healthy-seeming woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Well-nourished, well-hydrated. Affect pleasant and engaged.

Vital signs: Weight noted today — essentially at pre-treatment baseline (patient reports net loss of approximately 1-2 lbs from pre-treatment weight, considers this acceptable). Blood pressure within normal limits. Heart rate regular.

Head and Neck:
- Oral cavity/oropharynx: Mucosa well-healed. Residual mild mucosal pallor and mild atrophic changes at the right base of tongue region consistent with post-radiation sequelae. No erythema, no ulceration, no visible mass, no exophytic lesion. Saliva production subjectively reduced (consistent with xerostomia) but no thick ropy secretions obstructing the pharynx today. Teeth: existing dentition with evidence of prior extraction site (tooth #19) well-healed. Patient wearing fluoride trays as instructed.
- Flexible nasopharyngoscopy (performed by ENT co-management, results reviewed today): Well-healed mucosa at the right base of tongue. No mucosal irregularity, no submucosal fullness, no ulceration, no mass identified. Epiglottis and vallecula normal in appearance. Bilateral vocal cord mobility intact. No supraglottic or glottic lesions. Nasopharynx clear.
- Neck: No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally. No masses. Residual bilateral neck skin changes with mild post-radiation hyperpigmentation noted; no induration, no thickening suggestive of progressive subcutaneous fibrosis. Neck range of motion preserved. Carotid bruits not auscultated, though noted for future surveillance per radiation oncology recommendations.
- Thyroid: Not palpably enlarged. No nodules appreciated on examination.

Neurological: Cranial nerves grossly intact. No peripheral neuropathy reported (minimal or no Platinol-induced neuropathy).

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. PEG tube site healed, no evidence of wound complication or hernia.

---

LAB / ANCILLARY RESULTS

Thyroid function (most recent, obtained in advance of today's visit): TSH 2.1 mIU/L (within normal limits on adjusted levothyroxine dose — excellent response to dose titration). Free T4 normal.

Audiogram (obtained at 6-month mark, no repeat obtained today given stable symptoms): Bilateral mild sensorineural hearing loss, predominantly 4-8 kHz, consistent with cis ototoxicity. Does not meet criteria for hearing aid referral at this time. Will reassess annually per current practice.

Basic metabolic panel / CBC: Obtained at visit, results pending at time of dictation; no acute issues anticipated. Creatinine baseline was 0.8 mg/dL prior to treatment; last check at 6 months was 0.85 mg/dL, consistent with stable renal function following Platinol exposure.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT neck with contrast (12-month post-treatment surveillance): Reviewed and discussed in detail with the patient today. Findings notable for stable post-radiation changes in the oropharynx, including expected mucosal thickening and soft tissue stranding in the right base of tongue region, unchanged from prior post-treatment imaging. No discrete mass, no new enhancement pattern, no lesion concerning for recurrence. Right level IIA lymph node now measures 0.8 cm with homogeneous enhancement — dramatically decreased from 2.4 cm at diagnosis — consistent with complete treatment response and residual post-treatment fibrosis. No new or enlarging cervical lymphadenopathy. No concerning findings on the included chest/upper abdominal cuts. Overall impression: no evidence of disease recurrence at 12-month surveillance. This is consistent with the complete metabolic response documented on the 12-week post-treatment PET/CT.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-POSITIVE OROPHARYNGEAL SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, RIGHT BASE OF TONGUE, T2N1M0 — NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE, 12 MONTHS POST-TREATMENT

Ms. Chen has now reached the 12-month post-chemoradiation landmark with no clinical or radiographic evidence of disease. This is an excellent and expected outcome for a patient with HPV-positive, stage III oropharyngeal SCC who achieved complete metabolic response on post-treatment PET/CT. As discussed with her at length today, the 5-year disease-specific survival for patients with HPV-positive OPSCC treated with definitive cis-based chemoradiation who achieve complete response approaches 85-90% in many published series, and her trajectory remains very favorable. The 12-month disease-free milestone is an important one.

Surveillance plan per NCCN guidelines for HPV-positive OPSCC:
- History and physical examination (including flexible nasopharyngoscopy where clinically indicated) every 1-3 months during years 1-2 post-treatment. Given the favorable trajectory, next H&P in approximately 3 months.
- Imaging: CT neck with contrast or PET/CT at clinically indicated intervals; the next planned surveillance imaging will be at approximately 18 months post-treatment (CT neck with contrast vs. PET/CT at provider discretion). No PET/CT is specifically indicated at this interval given absence of clinical concern for recurrence.
- Patient counseled explicitly to call or present urgently for: new neck mass, new throat pain not explained by reflux or other benign cause, hemoptysis, otalgia (particularly unilateral), unexplained weight loss, or any new concerning symptoms.

2. cis-RELATED LATE EFFECTS — OTOTOXICITY / NEPHROTOXICITY
- Sensorineural hearing loss mild and stable. Annual audiogram going forward.
- Renal function stable. Ongoing monitoring with annual BMP. Patient advised to avoid nephrotoxic medications and ensure adequate hydration.

3. RADIATION-INDUCED HYPOTHYROIDISM
- TSH now normalized on adjusted levothyroxine dose. Continue current dose. TSH to be rechecked in approximately 6 months. Radiation oncology is aware; endocrinology co-management ongoing per patient preference.

4. XEROSTOMIA — LATE RADIATION EFFECT
- Persistent grade 1, well-managed with pilocarpine 5 mg TID, biotene rinses, saliva substitute spray, and frequent water intake. Patient coping well; this is not significantly impairing quality of life or work function. Recommend continuation of current regimen. Continued dental hygiene vigilance with nightly fluoride trays to reduce risk of radiation caries.

5. DYSPHAGIA — LATE RADIATION EFFECT, MILD RESIDUAL
- Patient tolerating regular diet with minor self-modifications. No clinical aspiration. No formal swallowing study obtained at this visit, as patient is clinically functional and without safety concerns. Will refer back to speech-language pathology if symptoms progress or if aspiration symptoms develop.

6. CAROTID VASCULOPATHY — RADIATION-INDUCED RISK
- Radiation to bilateral neck carries long-term risk of accelerated carotid atherosclerosis. Baseline carotid duplex ultrasound to be scheduled at the 5-year post-treatment mark, or sooner if clinical symptoms develop (TIA, stroke-like symptoms). Patient counseled on this risk and on cardiovascular risk factor management (blood pressure control, lipid screening, smoking avoidance — patient is a never-smoker).

7. OSTEORADIONECROSIS RISK — ONGOING DENTAL SURVEILLANCE
- Patient on fluoride tray program. Dental oncology follow-up every 6 months. Patient counseled on importance of reporting any jaw pain, dental pain, or exposed bone to her dental team promptly. No symptoms currently suggestive of ORN.

Return to clinic in approximately 3 months for routine surveillance visit. Labs to be reviewed at that time. Imaging at 18-month interval as above. Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and expressed gratitude for the care provided. She reports that returning to full-time teaching has been meaningful to her recovery and she is doing well overall.

Patient Education: Provided verbal counseling on signs/symptoms of recurrence, late radiation effects, and need for ongoing surveillance. Questions answered.

Time spent with patient and family: approximately 40 minutes, including review of imaging, discussion of prognosis, and counseling.
